metadata.visit_title: New high blood pressure — starting lisinopril

transcript:
DR: Thomas, thanks for coming back in for the recheck. We had you monitor your blood pressure at home over the last two weeks — how did the numbers look?
PT: Yeah, I wrote them down. Most mornings it was around 148 over 92, sometimes higher.
DR: That lines up with what we saw here. Consistently in that range, that's stage 2 high blood pressure, so this isn't a one-off.
PT: Okay. So it's official then.
DR: It is. The good news is it's very treatable. I'd like to start you on a medication called lisinopril, along with the diet and exercise changes we talked about.
PT: Lisinopril. What does it do?
DR: It relaxes your blood vessels so your heart doesn't have to push as hard. You'll take 10 milligrams once a day, in the morning. Same time each day is best.
PT: Ten milligrams, once in the morning. Any side effects I should know about?
DR: The most common one is a dry, tickly cough — if that shows up and it's annoying, tell me, because we can switch you to a different one. It's not dangerous, just bothersome.
PT: A dry cough, okay, I'll let you know.
DR: We'll also check your kidney function and potassium with a blood test in about two weeks, since this medication can affect those. The front desk will set that up.
PT: Blood test in two weeks, got it.
DR: Keep logging your morning readings too — bring the log to the next visit so we can see if the dose is doing its job.
PT: Will do. I've already cut way back on the salt like we said.
DR: That's great, keep that going. Let's plan to see you back in a month.
PT: One month. Sounds good.
DR: Now, one thing I need you to take seriously: this is rare, but if you ever notice swelling of your lips, tongue, face, or your throat feeling tight, or trouble breathing — stop the medication and get to an emergency room right away. Don't wait on that one.
PT: Swelling of the lips or tongue, or trouble breathing — go to the ER and stop the pill. Understood.
DR: Perfect. That's exactly it. Everything else, just call the office.
PT: Thanks, doctor. I appreciate it.
DR: You're welcome, Thomas. See you in a month.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Nguyen is a 47-year-old man presenting for blood-pressure recheck after two weeks of home monitoring. Home readings consistently ~148/92. No chest pain, dyspnea, headaches, or visual changes. Already reducing dietary salt.

**Objective:** BP confirmed in stage 2 hypertension range. Otherwise well.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- New essential hypertension, stage 2, confirmed by home readings. Start lisinopril 10 mg once daily in the morning. Counseled on possible dry cough (report if bothersome — can switch agents).
- Basic metabolic panel (renal function, potassium) in ~2 weeks.
- Continue lifestyle changes: low-sodium diet, exercise. Continue home BP logging.
- Return precautions: angioedema — swelling of lips/tongue/face, throat tightness, or difficulty breathing — stop medication and seek emergency care immediately.
- Follow-up in 1 month.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• New high blood pressure (stage 2)
• Starting a blood pressure medication

Next steps
• Start lisinopril 10 mg every morning.
• Get a blood test in about 2 weeks (kidney function and potassium).
• Keep logging your morning blood pressure and bring the log.
• Keep up the low-salt diet and exercise.
• Follow up in 1 month.
